draft.introduction: The Baldrs Draumar (“Baldr’s Dreams”) narrates Odin’s journey to Hel to learn the meaning of his son Baldr’s ominous dreams. By necromancy he raises a dead seeress who reveals that Baldr will be killed by his blind brother Hǫðr.

translation.text: Odin rode on to the eastern gate, where he knew the seeress’s grave to be. He began to speak a corpse-raising spell for the wise woman, until unwillingly she rose and spoke the words of the dead: “What man is this, unknown to me, who makes me travel the troublesome road?”
translation.original_text: Óðinn reið fyr austan dúrr, þar er hann vissi vǫlvu leiði; nam hann vittugri valgaldr kveða, unz nauðig reis, nás orð of kvað.

translator_notes.note: Odin’s necromancy (valgaldr, “corpse-spell”) demonstrates that even the chief god must resort to forbidden arts to obtain knowledge. The dead vǫlva’s resentment at being raised is a recurring motif—the dead possess knowledge the living need but do not wish to share it.
